### 2. Configuration

import logbook

def clize_log_level(*, logbook_level: 'll'="NOTICE"):

issue_title: Discussion about ffmpy features: progress event support
issue_body:
Hi,

I was working on a transcoding platform and I found your module to wrapping ffmpeg. I'm interested to make improvements that would be useful to everyone involved in niche. 

I'm planning to replace Popen.communicate call to achieve to goals:

- Make ffmpy efficient in memory usage
- Give it progress reporting support

Popen.communicate() is inefficient because of stdout and stderr buffering. Let's see some cases:

### Use case 1:
you want to send transcoded data over network as possible as available. If communicate() is used, you must to accumulate the entire transcoded movie on a memory buffer to send it.

### Use case 2:
you want to know the transcoding state (output size, time, bitrate) in real time. If communicate() is used, you can't do it because this method is consuming the stdout pipe!

I have a experimental branch with a on_progress callback implemented here:
https://github.com/astroza/ffmpy/tree/progress_support

An usage example is:

```python
def on_progress(state):
        print state.time

ff = ffmpy.FFmpeg(
        global_options="-hide_banner -nostdin -y -stats",
        inputs={local_input_path: None},
        outputs={local_output_path: None},
        on_progress=on_progress
)
ff.run(stderr=subprocess.PIPE)
```

Now I'm working to make it backwards compatible. The branch break your API

issue_title: Is this project still under active development?
issue_body: ipdb is a great tool and I use it every day, but the last PR merged was on Jun 14, 2016. There are currently four (well really three because two of them fix the same bug) open PRs that need attention.

I think the empty byte string `b''` should encode to the empty string `""`. Instead, it currently encodes to `"11"`.

Similarly, the string `"1"` should decode to the byte `b'\x00'`. Instead, it currently decodes to `b'\x00\x00'`.

I'm not totally sure about the right behavior for Base58 padding, but I think at least it should be possible to round-trip any string of bytes. Right now if you send any number of zero-bytes (including the empty bytestring) through encode+decode, you get a different number of bytes back out.
Кажется что запрет на изменение конфига в runtime это лишнее. Это вряд ли предотвратит большое количество ошибок и выглядит не по питонячьи.

Hello all.

I understand this isn't the most bristling-with-activity project, but -- coming at it from the perspective of a Python tinkerer who's fallen in love with R's tidyverse -- this is really clever stuff. I've been reading through the source code and it's clear, pretty, and concise.

It is, however, deeply stitched up to Pandas. One of the neat things about dplyr / the tidyverse in general is that it works (to a greater or lesser degree!) with other sources like DBI connections or Spark. Further, it allows the programmer to bring an almost declarative paradigm where it might be warranted to tasks unrelated to data wrangling. I think some Pythonistas would frown deeply at the suggestion, but something like dfply's pipes with an FP library like toolz could be really nice from my perspective.

All that said: would you be amenable to my taking on the task of trying to generalize dfply a little? Obviously the tidyverse would be too huge an undertaking, but I could see making the pipe code more general or in the medium term something like a proof-of-concept SQL generator or SQLalchemy backend.

issue_title: Question on defining facts and flow structure
issue_body:
Hi @jruizgit - thanks so much for your work on this! It's very well documented and the architecture seems easy to bring into an existing project.

I'm still new to and learning about rete, but this seems like a system we'll need to adopt. I've been able to get a number of your examples up and running, but I could use some help in understanding how to lay out different facts in our system. In particular, when there might be many different types of facts (vs just a single stream of transaction events, for example).

A simple example that aligns with my project is an Amazon warehouse. There are boxes to be shipped and places they need to be stored in the warehouse as they move through process.

- Boxes might have facts like dimensions, weight, fragile or not, temperature requirements, shipping date, etc.
- The warehouse stations would have facts like current capacity/usage, location, cold room or not, perhaps shipping date ranges for boxes within it, etc.

Could I have multiple fact types like this that are both evaluated in my rules? Example, if the current box being processed requires a cold room and the cold room has capacity, move it there next to boxes with the same shipping date.

Any clarification on this use-case and flow structure to be used would be a big help. Thank you again!

issue_title: Support signing data that has a pre-existing signature somewhere
issue_body:
The signxml._get_signature_regex() function is used to pull signature blocks out of the post-canonicalized data using a regex. When signing a payload that includes another signature, this function removes the internal signatures as well. 

The end result is that the internal, original signatures remain valid but the outer, new signature created by signxml omits the inner signature blocks from the digest and is invalid

issue_title: Incorrect denormalization when nested Records are queried
issue_body:
# Short description

When the graph contains nested fields of type `Record`, validation considers the query which requests specific fields of nested record, invalid.

# Reproducing

## Environment

```
Python 3.6.4
```

## Dependencies
(obtained by `pip install hiku graphql-core`)

```
graphql-core==2.0
Hiku==0.4.1
promise==2.1
Rx==1.6.0
six==1.11.0
typing==3.6.2
```

## Code

```python
from hiku.graph import Graph, Root, Field
from hiku.types import Record, String, Integer

from hiku.readers.graphql import read
from hiku.engine import Engine
from hiku.result import denormalize
from hiku.executors.sync import SyncExecutor


def config_resolver(fields):
    return [{
        'app': {
            'host': 'trunk.example.com',
            'port': 80,
        },
    }]


AppConfig = Record[{'host': String, 'port': Integer}]
Config = Record[{'app': AppConfig}]

graph = Graph([
    Root([
        Field('config', Config, config_resolver),
    ])
])

hiku_engine = Engine(SyncExecutor())


query = '''
  query {
    config {
      app { host }
    }
  }
'''
query = read(query)

result = hiku_engine.execute(graph, query)
simple_result = denormalize(graph, result, query)
print(simple_result)

assert simple_result == {'config': {'app': {'host': 'trunk.example.com'}}}

```

## Actual result

`port` field is included in result and assertion fails.

```
{'config': {'app': {'host': 'trunk.example.com', 'port': 80}}}
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "graph.py", line 44, in <module>
    assert simple_result == {'config': {'app': {'host': 'trunk.example.com'}}}
AssertionError
```

## Expected result

`port` field is not present in the result, thus result having the following shape:

```
{'config': {'app': {'host': 'trunk.example.com'}}}
```

issue_title: Failure with completion for read() on ZipFile: "RuntimeError: maximum recursion depth exceeded"
issue_body:
I've tried to complete after `read()` of a `zipfile.Zipfile`, which took very long:

```
import jedi
s="import zipfile; z = zipfile.ZipFile(\"foo\"); z.read(n)."
jedi.Script(s, 1, len(s), ".").completions()
```

Using `strace`, it looks like it tries to open everything / a lot from the import path?!

```
% strace -f -eopen python -c 'import jedi; s="import zipfile; z = zipfile.ZipFile(\"foo\"); z.read(n)."; print(jedi.Script(s, 1, len(s), ".").completions())'
```

In the end it aborts with `RuntimeError: maximum recursion depth exceeded`.

I hope I got the arguments to `jedi.Script` right (`len(s)`).

This is more of a question than an issue --
I'm wondering if the scenario of using pycparser in another project as a git submodule (without installing anything) is "officialy" supported, or whether I should expect things to break with future versions.

In general, submodules are preferred to installed libraries, because they are self-contained and behave more consistently (for starters, you know exactly which version the user is going to get)

Thanks.

I am classifying 3 million texts, and usually classification performance is good. However, for a few texts that are all uppercase, performance is very poor. Here is a toy example

````
>>> import langid
>>> print langid.classify('The quick brown fox jumps over the lazy dog')
('en', 0.9999999999138445)
>>> print langid.classify('THE QUICK BROWN FOX JUMPS OVER THE LAZY DOG')

Also, all lowercase text can have poor performance
````
>>> print langid.classify('I do not speak English. Do you?')
('en', 0.9071235348010346)
>>> print langid.classify('i do not speak English. do you?')
('pt', 0.9283325957561123)
````

Do you think langid should be retrained on all-capitalized and all-lowercase examples? This is analogous to how image classification training sets are augmented by distorting the original images through transformations such as mirroring or warping.

issue_title: Producer doesn't reconnect if broker goes down, reappears with new IP
issue_body:
I'm using Docker swarm to manage Kafka brokers, so if a broker goes down, swarm will automatically create a new instance in new container. The catch is that the new container will likely have a different IP address from the old one. The issue is this: once that new broker comes up, kafka-python will never connect to it.

Here's an example running on my local machine, where I'm running a single node that can be discovered with 'kafka.local'. I run one Kafka broker which magically sets its advertised.listeners to the container's IP, which in this case was 172.17.0.4 for the old container and 172.17.0.5 for the new one. (both Kakfa and Zookeeper are in the same container, but I'm using a volume to persist their data so clients think it's the same broker when it finally comes back up)

Logs follow.

First, the node goes down and you see a lot of this:
```
2017-04-24T02:55:27.652Z conn.py:211 DEBUG:kafka.conn: <BrokerConnection host=172.17.0.4/172.17.0.4 port=9092>: creating new socket
2017-04-24T02:55:27.652Z sender.py:109 DEBUG:kafka.producer.sender: Node 0 not ready; delaying produce of accumulated batch
2017-04-24T02:55:27.653Z conn.py:295 ERROR:kafka.conn: Connect attempt to <BrokerConnection host=172.17.0.4/172.17.0.4 port=9092> returned error 111. Disconnecting.
2017-04-24T02:55:27.653Z client_async.py:304 WARNING:kafka.client: Node 0 connection failed -- refreshing metadata
2017-04-24T02:55:27.654Z conn.py:211 DEBUG:kafka.conn: <BrokerConnection host=172.17.0.4/172.17.0.4 port=9092>: creating new socket
2017-04-24T02:55:27.655Z sender.py:109 DEBUG:kafka.producer.sender: Node 0 not ready; delaying produce of accumulated batch
2017-04-24T02:55:27.655Z conn.py:295 ERROR:kafka.conn: Connect attempt to <BrokerConnection host=172.17.0.4/172.17.0.4 port=9092> returned error 111. Disconnecting.
2017-04-24T02:55:27.656Z client_async.py:304 WARNING:kafka.client: Node 0 connection failed -- refreshing metadata
2017-04-24T02:55:27.656Z client_async.py:656 ERROR:kafka.client: No nodes found in metadata -- retrying bootstrap
2017-04-24T02:55:27.656Z client_async.py:213 DEBUG:kafka.client: Sleeping 12.6888 before bootstrapping again
```
Meanwhile, we send more messages:
```
2017-04-24T02:55:30.202Z kafka.py:508 DEBUG:kafka.producer.kafka: Sending (key=1 value=1) to TopicPartition(topic='_test-count', partition=0)
```
kafka-python realizes something's terribly wrong and tries bootstrapping again using the original bootstrap_server value, which is good:
```
2017-04-24T02:55:40.355Z client_async.py:223 DEBUG:kafka.client: Attempting to bootstrap via node at kafka.local:9092
2017-04-24T02:55:40.357Z conn.py:211 DEBUG:kafka.conn: <BrokerConnection host=kafka.local/kafka.local port=9092>: creating new socket
2017-04-24T02:55:40.358Z conn.py:281 DEBUG:kafka.conn: <BrokerConnection host=kafka.local/127.0.0.1 port=9092>: established TCP connection
2017-04-24T02:55:40.359Z client_async.py:274 DEBUG:kafka.client: Node bootstrap connected
2017-04-24T02:55:40.362Z conn.py:545 DEBUG:kafka.conn: <BrokerConnection host=kafka.local/127.0.0.1 port=9092> Request 1: MetadataRequest_v1(topics=NULL)
2017-04-24T02:55:40.387Z conn.py:721 DEBUG:kafka.conn: <BrokerConnection host=kafka.local/127.0.0.1 port=9092> Response 1: MetadataResponse_v1(brokers=[(node_id=0, host='172.17.0.5', port=9092, rack=None)], controller_id=0, topics=[(error_code=0, topic='_test-count', is_internal=False, partitions=[(error_code=0, partition=0, leader=0, replicas=[0], isr=[0])]), (error_code=0, topic='aqi-ground-station-raw-data', is_internal=False, partitions=[(error_code=0, partition=0, leader=0, replicas=[0], isr=[0])])])
2017-04-24T02:55:40.388Z cluster.py:272 DEBUG:kafka.cluster: Updated cluster metadata to ClusterMetadata(brokers: 1, topics: 2, groups: 0)
```
It's successful, but the client object doesn't seem to notice:
```
2017-04-24T02:55:40.389Z client_async.py:707 DEBUG:kafka.client: Give up sending metadata request since no node is available
```
And continues doing this forever:
```
2017-04-24T02:55:40.391Z conn.py:211 DEBUG:kafka.conn: <BrokerConnection host=172.17.0.4/172.17.0.4 port=9092>: creating new socket
2017-04-24T02:55:40.392Z sender.py:109 DEBUG:kafka.producer.sender: Node 0 not ready; delaying produce of accumulated batch
2017-04-24T02:55:40.392Z conn.py:295 ERROR:kafka.conn: Connect attempt to <BrokerConnection host=172.17.0.4/172.17.0.4 port=9092> returned error 111. Disconnecting.
2017-04-24T02:55:40.393Z client_async.py:304 WARNING:kafka.client: Node 0 connection failed -- refreshing metadata
2017-04-24T02:55:40.394Z conn.py:211 DEBUG:kafka.conn: <BrokerConnection host=172.17.0.4/172.17.0.4 port=9092>: creating new socket
2017-04-24T02:55:40.395Z sender.py:109 DEBUG:kafka.producer.sender: Node 0 not ready; delaying produce of accumulated batch
2017-04-24T02:55:40.396Z conn.py:295 ERROR:kafka.conn: Connect attempt to <BrokerConnection host=172.17.0.4/172.17.0.4 port=9092> returned error 111. Disconnecting.
```
Another variation of this bug happens when, during bootstrap, the name in bootstrap_servers fails to resolve (socket.gaierror) and comes back up later. The reconnect logic seems complicated though, so I doubt the same fix would work for that.

This is a ship stopper for me (and should be for anyone else who's running Kakfa in docker swarm) so I'm going to start digging in and come up with a fix. Any help would be appreciated though!

Currently when encountering an object, `DeepSearch` examines that object's `__dict__` without testing whether the object is what's being searched for. By example:

```
>>> from deepdiff import DeepSearch
>>> from uuid import uuid4()
>>> foo = uuid4()
>>> DeepSearch({1: foo}, foo)
{}
```

This would also allow global singletons like `None` and functions to be searched for.

When I send a lot of messages over a hardware CAN bus running at 250.000 bit/s, some problems arise when the messages are sent in a very fast pace:

    for n in range(0, 10000):
        message = can.Message(arbitration_id=42, data=bytearray([1, 2, 3]))
        sender.send(message)

Obviously the CAN bus itself cannot handle all these messages that fast, so its understandable that about 80% of the messages are dropped (this obviously varies a lot). But wouldn't it be great if the library would buffer the send requests in a queue and dispatch them at a rate the can can handle? Because even when sending just 30 messages (a more realistic scenario), somewhere between 2 and 3 messages are dropped. 

There is a dirty solution that I implemented for now:

    for n in range(0, 10000):
        message = can.Message(arbitration_id=42, data=bytearray([1, 2, 3]))
        sender.send(message)
        time.sleep(1.0 / 2000) # sleep a little bit, else the CAN is overwhelmed

But I do not think that this is a task for the user of the library, but should instead be handled by python-can itself. I do not think putting a delay of 0.5 ms in there would be a good idea, but rather a queue on a separate thread, or at lease give the option to do so. And that thread would try to send the messages and retry on failure, dispatching all waiting messages one by one. 

What do you think about this, or is that even implemented somewhere?

issue_title: A complex generator of a lot of dates.
issue_body:
Sometimes we need to generate a lot of `datetime` objects and at this moment we have not a good solution for this, so I suggest something like this:

```python
from datetime import datetime, timedelta

def bulk_create_datetimes(diapason=(), *args, **kwargs):
    dt_objects = []
    # Maybe that's better to raise an exception 
    # instead of using default range?
    try:
        start, end = diapason
    except ValueError:
        end = datetime.now()
        start = end - timedelta(days=7)

    while start <= end:
        start += timedelta(*args, **kwargs)
        dt_objects.append(start)
    return dt_objects
```

How it works:

```python
>>> week_ago = datetime.now() - timedelta(days=7)
>>> now = datetime.now()
>>> datetimes = bulk_create_datetimes(diapason=(week_ago, now), minutes=60)
>>> for i in datetimes:
....    print(i)
```
Result:
```
2018-08-21 12:58:55.687991
2018-08-21 13:58:55.687991
2018-08-21 14:58:55.687991
2018-08-21 15:58:55.687991
2018-08-21 16:58:55.687991
...
2018-08-28 12:58:55.687991
```

So, I think that it's pretty simple to understand and there is no need for comments.

That gives extreme flexibility in generating dates because a user can generate dates with any difference between them (including seconds, minutes and etc.).

I believe there is an issue the way user-input is processed. Specifically, I am concerned about the passphrase. The input passphrase is passed along to the execution module `Naked.toolshed.shell.execute()` without any safety checks. The code either uses `Naked.toolshed.shell.execute()` or `Naked.toolshed.shell.muterun()` to run all gpg commands.

The problem with this is that `execute()`/`muterun()` apparently will trigger `subprocess.call`/`subprocess.check_output` with `shell=True`. This is a relevant security problem as the user could (accidentally or not) execute any shell command this way. This exact scenario is actually documented here:
https://docs.python.org/2/library/subprocess.html#frequently-used-arguments

I am not sure if this is all still true in Python 3 or if getpass does anything special (I don't think so, other than not displaying what the user types). This should probably be addressed somehow, though.

issue_title: Change ChatBot filters parameter to import classes from the string path
issue_body:
Currently, filters classes are being set like this:

```python
ChatBot(
    # ...
    filters=[
        FilterClass1,
        FilterClass2
    ]
)
```

They should be changed to use the string import path to the module like all of the other module parameters:

```python
ChatBot(
    # ...
    filters=[
        'path.to.filter.module.FilterClass1',
        'path.to.filter.module.FilterClass2'
    ]
)
```

This change should be made before the next release since it will be the first release including the filter class options.

What I have:
```
{'item': [{'@n': 'LEVEL_1',
           '@v': {'item': [{'@n': 'KEY_2', '@v': 'VAL_2', '@x': 0},
                           {'@n': 'KEY_3', '@v': 'VAL_3', '@x': 0},
                           {'@n': 'KEY_1', '@v': 'VAL_1', '@x': 0}]},
           '@x': 1}]}
```

```
<item
    n="LEVEL_1"
    x=1
    v="
        <item n="KEY_1" v="VAL_1" x=0 />
        <item n="KEY_2" v="VAL_2" x=0 />
        <item n="KEY_3" v="VAL_3" x=0 />
    "
/>
```

issue_title: Specifying the search path for FileSystemLoader
issue_body:
I have this directory structure on current workdir:

```
.
└── service
    ├── base.yaml
    ├── external
    │   └── base.yaml
    └── internal
        └── base.yaml
```

The `./service/internal/base.yaml` has a `{% extends "./service/base.yaml" %}` which works correctly if I run the jinja2 module manually with:

```py
from jinja2 import Environment, FileSystemLoader, select_autoescape
env = Environment(loader=FileSystemLoader('.'))
env.get_template('service/internal/base.yaml').render()
```

But running `jinja2` CLI fails:


```
$ jinja2 service/external/base.yaml
...
jinja2.exceptions.TemplateNotFound: service/base.yaml
```

I think `jinja2` tool needs to use the current workdir ($CWD) as the search directory (or have a --flag to signal a /path/to/templates).

issue_title: M2M relationships are not saved in associated version (they appear on subsequent saves)
issue_body:
Version 2.0.9
Django 1.8.9
I'm seeing an issue where changes to a many-to-many field made through the django admin interface are not saved in the version record. For example given the following classes:

```
class Users:
    pass

class Computers:
    users = models.ManyToManyField(User, related_name='computers', blank=True)
```

 I register them via:

```
class UserAdminModel(VersionAdmin):
    def reversion_register(self, model, **options):
        options["follow"] = ("computers",)
        super(UserAdminModel, self).reversion_register(model, **options)

class ComputerAdminModel(VersionAdmin):
    def reversion_register(self, model, **options):
        options["follow"] = ("users",)
        super(ComputerAdminModel, self).reversion_register(model, **options)
```

If I use the m2m "filter_horizontal" widget on the user edit page and change the computers they are assigned to, it is reflected in the saved object but not the one in the history list. Its worth noting that this problem does not happen in reverse i.e. when changing the m2m relationship from the computers screen. I believe this issue is directly related to https://groups.google.com/forum/#!topic/django-reversion/gg9q65LuX7Q and the open issue linked to in the first comment (for django-reversion-compare although I believe the problem exists within django-reversion).

issue_title: Can't decrypt AES256 encrypted binary file
issue_body:
Hi, somehow I'm unable to decrypt a binary file through python-gnupg. A few month ago this still worked (but I can't remember the exact gnupg-version):

```
#!/usr/bin/env python3
# -*- coding: utf-8 -*-

import os
import gnupg

def main():
    home = os.path.expanduser('~')
    homedir = os.path.join(home, ".gnupg")
    binary = "/usr/bin/gpg2"

    passphrase = ',S!a{`\'M[r%.&#4}vS9Uu^+p&.Q4C;1n'

    gpg = gnupg.GPG(binary=binary, homedir=homedir)

    with open("IYThDruNGyc5iaePDEtKJYCVDTGadj7d", "rb") as fIn:
        dec = gpg.decrypt(fIn.read(), passphrase=passphrase)
        print(dec.data)

if __name__ == '__main__':
    main()
```

Instead of a successfully decrypted file it just throws an error (but, unfortunately, doesn't abort the program)

```
$ ./decrypt-file.py 
Exception in thread Thread-2:
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "/usr/lib/python3.4/threading.py", line 920, in _bootstrap_inner
    self.run()
  File "/usr/lib/python3.4/threading.py", line 868, in run
    self._target(*self._args, **self._kwargs)

    result._handle_status(keyword, value)
  File "/usr/lib/python3.4/site-packages/gnupg/_parsers.py", line 1506, in _handle_status
    super(Crypt, self)._handle_status(key, value)
  File "/usr/lib/python3.4/site-packages/gnupg/_parsers.py", line 1419, in _handle_status
    raise ValueError("Unknown status message: %r" % key)
ValueError: Unknown status message: 'FAILURE'

b''
$
```

I'm able to decrypt the file if I'm using gpg (without the python stuff): `gpg -d IYThDruNGyc5iaePDEtKJYCVDTGadj7d # works`

This was tested on an up to date Arch Linux installation, using gpg 2.1.8 and libgcrypt 1.6.4, python-gnupg was installed through this package: https://aur.archlinux.org/packages/python-gnupg-isis/

Any suggestions?

issue_title: Fetching a list of automated emails is missing query params
issue_body:
For some reason, Mailchimp API v3 official documentation doesn't mention the usual query params for this particular API endpoint:  `/automations/{workflow_id}/emails`.

So in the corresponding mailchimp3 library call: `client.automations.emails.all(workflow_id='')`, it accepts neither pagination query params nor field related filters(`fields`, `exclude_fields`). 

As a result, the results returned from mailchimp are using the default 10 item page. If you have more than 10 automation campaign emails, it will only return the first 10.

I spoke with Mailchimp support, the pagination and field filter query params are global for all the GET calls. 

I will try to come up with a PR for this.

issue_title: Prepare for Python Markdown inline processor changes
issue_body:
I am currently reworking inline processor in a new InlineProcessor class for Python Markdown which improves inline performance, but it also adds the ability to do more analysis and alter the bounds of the original match (ref https://github.com/Python-Markdown/markdown/issues/551).  This is much more flexible. For example, we can match just `[` for links, and then in the plugin tokenize the next characters handling recursive `[]` which Python's re cannot handle.  Python Markdown is moving all inline processors in that direction and providing the legacy versions of the old methods for the basic patterns, but there are two `InlineProcessors` we use that are not going to be accessible after the change `LinkPattern` and `HtmlPattern`.  So we need to simply prepare by checking now whether we can import `LinkPattern` in MagicLinks, and `HtmlPattern` in SmartSymbols.

We will provide alternate logic for these two cases (import succeeds and import fails).  All other patterns can use legacy logic for now. After the Python Markdown finally releases the change, we can change all of pymdown-extensions to utilize the new `InlineProcessor` classes and drop the `Pattern` classes.  Then we can lock pymdown-extensions to whatever version of Markdown that introduces the new `InlineProcessor` classes.

So basically there are two phases:

- [x] Compatibility phase -- do the bare minimum to keep working (make sure we work now, and once the changes are released)
- [ ] Full implementation phase (we fully use the new InlineProcessor class and abandon Pattern).

issue_body:
I've stumbled upon a design decision, I don't entirely understand, while re-implementing EmbeddedDocumentSerializer.

In test_basic.py::TestIntegration we work with [a model RegularModel with CustomField](https://github.com/umutbozkurt/django-rest-framework-mongoengine/blob/master/tests/test_basic.py#L58).

Respective `DocumentSerializer` automatically creates `DocumentField`, corresponding to `CustomField`. When DRF creates `validated_data` for `DocumentSerializer` by calling `DocumentSerialier.is_valid()`, no key/value pair is created in `validated_data` for this `DocumentField`, cause it provides no `run_validation()` method.

I think, that all the statically declared fields should have their values in `validated_data` by the time `DocumentSerializer.to_internal_value()` is called. These values either derive from `run_validation()` call, or from the context, passed to `DocumentSerializer.save()`, which, in turn, calls `DocumentSerializer.create/update()`, which calls `DocumentSerializer.to_internal_value()`.

What do you think about this suggestion? Do I miss something?

I use the following code to construct a grouped symbol,got the Error:
symbol.cc:489: Check failed: node == e.source.get() Symbol.GetName only works for non-grouped symbol

```
def get_lenet():
    data = mx.symbol.Variable('data')
    # first conv
    conv1 = mx.symbol.Convolution(data=data, kernel=(5,5), num_filter=20)
    tanh1 = mx.symbol.Activation(data=conv1, act_type="tanh")
    pool1 = mx.symbol.Pooling(data=tanh1, pool_type="max",
                              kernel=(2,2), stride=(2,2))
    # second conv
    conv2 = mx.symbol.Convolution(data=pool1, kernel=(5,5), num_filter=50)
    tanh2 = mx.symbol.Activation(data=conv2, act_type="tanh")
    pool2 = mx.symbol.Pooling(data=tanh2, pool_type="max",
                              kernel=(2,2), stride=(2,2))

    _, out_shapes, _ = pool2.infer_shape(data=(1,1,28,28))
    softmax_class_1 = mx.symbol.Convolution(data=pool2, num_filter=10 + 1,
                                           kernel=(5, 5), stride=(1, 1), pad=(2, 2), name='softmax_class_1')
    _, out_shapes, _ = softmax_class_1.infer_shape(data=(1,1,28,28))
    shape = out_shapes[0][2:]

    softmax_avg_pool = mx.symbol.Pooling(data=softmax_class_1, kernel=(shape[0], 1), stride=(shape[0], 1),
                                         pad=(0, 0), pool_type='avg', name='softmax_avg_pool')

    softmax_class = mx.symbol.SoftmaxActivation(data=softmax_avg_pool, mode='channel', name='softmax_class')
    make_loss = mx.symbol.MakeLoss(data=softmax_class, grad_scale=0.0)

    ctc_label = mx.symbol.Cast(data=mx.symbol.Variable('softmax_label'),dtype='int32')
    ctc_loss = mx.symbol.CTCLoss(data=softmax_class, label=ctc_label, max_label_length=1,
                                name='ctc_class')
    return mx.sym.Group([make_loss, ctc_loss])

issue_title: pyOpenSSL and service_identify should be in an extras_require

Since fido doesn't actually import anything from pyOpenSSL and is meant to be installed as a library, we shouldn't be depending on it as an install_requires.

Instead we should use an extras_requires (see [docs](http://setuptools.readthedocs.io/en/latest/setuptools.html#new-and-changed-setup-keywords))

I'm attempting to use parameter binding in Impyla and I think there's a bug in the escaping [here](../blob/master/impala/interface.py#L240) and [here](../blob/master/impala/interface.py#L226). When attempting to perform a query with substitution, such as: `SELECT * FROM table_name WHERE %(thing)s > 100`, the query produced by Impyla ends up like: `SELECT * FROM table_name WHERE 'column_name' > 100`. Impala then complains that strings and ints can't be compared (understandably). I think the problem is that column names are identifiers, whereas single quoted strings are literals. I poked at the code and found the two lines linked above, which seem to be the culprits. Query parameters are indiscriminately being quoted, which you don't always want.

Am I wrong or is this a bug? If so, I'd be willing to try to submit a patch.

Brief: It would be advantageous if we had greater control over the dropout that gets applied to each layer in our model.

After presenting on the paper 'Swapout: Learning an ensemble of deep architectures'[0], I performed some experimentation to see the results of differing levels of dropout, ResNet, swapout, etc. on the DeepSpeech model. An important part of the positive results in the swapout paper was applying a linearly-increasing amount of dropout depending on the depth of the layers. This makes intuitive sense, as any stochasticity applied at the top of the model will have an exponentially increasing effect as it travels deeper into the tree.

Performing tests with a limited sample of the TED data set, it would appear that a dropout of lin(0.1,0.4) (i.e. dropouts increasing linearly from 0.1 to 0.4 on the feed-forward layers of the network) outperforms dropout of 0.3, when measuring validation loss and WER. Further testing also showed that applying dropout of 0.2 on the RNN layers of the network also had positive effects on validation loss and WER.

While further experimentation is needed to determine optimal values, it would be advantageous to have the parameters to configure and test these values in our implementation.

[0] https://docs.google.com/presentation/d/1dyl7F_sW0YLa3HchHVwugdBlvFzwbWRSiCRjD-iX2Yw/edit?usp=sharing

One of the things I've been thinking about recently is how notification dicts ought to be made, and then routed to their appropriate event handlers. At the moment `_notify_trait` receives several arguments from a `TraitType`, constructs a change dict, and then distributes it to `notify_change`. In the future, it seems like making `_notify_trait` responsible for constructing these notification dicts will make it difficult to generalize this process for events other than changes. It makes more sense to me that `TraitTypes` would be responsible for constructing dicts such that `_notify_trait` would know how to route them.

issue_title: vector values are truncated to ints
issue_body:
I did a `sudo pip install` of annoy on my OSX laptop and had the following unusual results:

```
>>> from annoy import AnnoyIndex
>>> import random
>>>
>>> f = 2
>>> t = AnnoyIndex(f)
>>> t.add_item(0, [0, 2.5])
0
>>> t.add_item(1, [3.5, 0])
0
>>> t.build(10)
0
>>> t.get_item_vector(0)
[0, 2]
>>> t.get_item_vector(1)
[3, 0]
>>> t.get_distance(0,1)
2.0
```

A few issues:
* A 0 is returned and printed by the add_item() and build() methods.
* When the items are queried, the vector that is returned has truncated elements.
* The get_distance() method doesn't return the proper angular distance, nor the distance under the assumption that the vector elements are truncated.

issue_title: Comprehension variables leaked as top-level symbols
issue_body:
Originally raised in lgeiger/ide-python#108.  Any variables within a list comprehension are considered to have `container: null` when they are actually contained by a list comprehension.

Minimal examples that cause the problem:
```{python}
[i for i in 'foo']  # i leaked
def bar(): return [j for j in 'baz']  # j leaked in both the single- and multiline versions of this function
{spam: eggs for spam, eggs in [("hovercraft", "eels")]} # spam, eggs leaked
{i for i in range(10)}  # i leaked again
```

issue_title: Pathlib TextIOWrapper not accepted for Lambda ZipFile Upload
issue_body:
zip_path is a Path object.
```
with zip_path.open() as zip_file:
    response = lambda_client.update_function_code(
        FunctionName=self.settings['function-name'],
        ZipFile=zip_file.read(),
        Publish=self.publish,
    )
```
This returns ``UnicodeDecodeError: 'utf8' codec can't decode byte 0xc4 in position 10: invalid continuation byte``

But if I change the code to use a built-in ``open``, then it will work:
```
with open(str(zip_path)) as zip_file:
    response = lambda_client.update_function_code(
        FunctionName=self.settings['function-name'],
        ZipFile=zip_file.read(),
        Publish=self.publish,
    )
```

issue_title: Automatically set the unicode flag when necessary.
issue_body:
By adding a GSM encoding to `codecs`, we could detect when encoding would fail on the server, and automatically switch on the unicode flag.

Instructions for adding a gsm encoding: https://stackoverflow.com/questions/37775209/how-to-create-a-gsm-7-encoding-in-python

TBH we probably don't even need to go this far - simply having a set of allowed characters and mapping against that would allow us to detect characters outside the GSM character set.

issue_title: Make a licensing note somewhere about `pipenv check`
issue_body:
While writing https://github.com/pyupio/safety-db/pull/2261, I realised that commercial redistributors of `pipenv` should take close note of the fact that `pipenv check` relies on a CC-BY-NC-SA CVE database maintained by pyup.io: https://github.com/pyupio/safety-db

This means that commercial redistributors of `pipenv` need to choose between:

* disabling `pipenv check`;
* patching `pipenv` to use a different vulnerability database (e.g. one they maintain themselves); or
* paying `pyup.io` for a commercial usage license

It took me a while to realize that I did not need to include system libraries like `json`, `logging` or `os` in the Pipfile. For someone new to virtualenvs and package management, a simple note in the docs or during install telling me these are not needed would have been very helpful.

It would be great if `python3 -m pipenv` worked in order to support Windows users better as neither `pipsi` nor `--user` installs are accessible by default. As it currently stands there's a message about `pew` needing to be on the `PATH`, although there's an [environment variable](https://docs.pipenv.org/advanced/#configuration-with-environment-variables) called `PIPENV_VENV_IN_PROJECT` which doesn't seem to have a corresponding command-line flag (I also didn't think to test if this removed the `pew` warning before leaving work today).

issue_title: js-yaml timestamp regexp is more liberal than the standard
issue_body:
From http://yaml.org/type/timestamp.html
> Regexp:
> ```
 [0-9][0-9][0-9][0-9]-[0-9][0-9]-[0-9][0-9] # (ymd)
|[0-9][0-9][0-9][0-9] # (year)
 -[0-9][0-9]? # (month)
 -[0-9][0-9]? # (day)
 ([Tt]|[ \t]+)[0-9][0-9]? # (hour)
 :[0-9][0-9] # (minute)
 :[0-9][0-9] # (second)
 (\.[0-9]*)? # (fraction)
 (([ \t]*)Z|[-+][0-9][0-9]?(:[0-9][0-9])?)? # (time zone)
```

The first form (ymd) does not allow single digit days or months, but the regex in [`js-yaml/type/timestamp` ](https://github.com/nodeca/js-yaml/blob/5f058c3a78fd1a1bd36fd2cdc2c3a8c45ab53fd1/lib/js-yaml/type/timestamp.js#L5) will allow this.

Ideal to (re-)test SELECTs, INSERTs (with autoincrementing primary keys), UPDATEs, and DELETEs with:

* SQLite
* MySQL
* PostgreSQL

PostgreSQL, in particular, seems not to support [`lastrowid`](http://docs.sqlalchemy.org/en/latest/core/connections.html#sqlalchemy.engine.ResultProxy.lastrowid) (cf. http://stackoverflow.com/a/2189662), so I suspect this condition won't catch INSERTs in the same way:

```python
# if INSERT, return primary key value for a newly inserted row
elif result.lastrowid is not None:
    return result.lastrowid
```

Ergo the parenthetical comments:

```python
# if SELECT (or INSERT with RETURNING), return result set as list of dict objects
...
# if DELETE or UPDATE (or INSERT without RETURNING), return number of rows matched
```

Goal, then, is to test whether `execute` indeed behaves exactly as the comments and return values imply:

```python
# if SELECT (or INSERT with RETURNING), return result set as list of dict objects
if result.returns_rows:
    rows = result.fetchall()
    return [dict(row) for row in rows]

# if INSERT, return primary key value for a newly inserted row
elif result.lastrowid is not None:
    return result.lastrowid

# if DELETE or UPDATE (or INSERT without RETURNING), return number of rows matched
else:
    return result.rowcount
```

Seems reasonable, though, if user is user PostgreSQL, to expect them to use `RETURNING id` in order to get back INSERTs' autoincremented IDs, a la http://stackoverflow.com/a/8590931.

CC @glennholloway @brianyu28

Some options, such as `minSize` are now hardcoded (`minSize` is set to `[5, 5]` for example). It's better to avoid that and add possibility to set it via settings.

The Invenio-Records module tables bear a "traditional" name in singular, i.e. `record`. It may be good to profit from migration to Invenio v3.0 in order to standardise table names and module names globally.  E.g. the currently created Invenio v3.0 tables are:

```
invenio3=# \dt 
               List of relations
 Schema |       Name        | Type  |  Owner
--------+-------------------+-------+----------
 public | accounts_role     | table | invenio3
 public | accounts_user     | table | invenio3
 public | accounts_userrole | table | invenio3
 public | pidLOG            | table | invenio3
 public | pidSTORE          | table | invenio3
 public | record            | table | invenio3
 public | record_version    | table | invenio3
 public | transaction       | table | invenio3
(8 rows)
```

Note that pidSTORE and pidLOG is better to be expressed in all lowercase for easy PostgreSQL compliance. This seems to be already in the target of https://github.com/inveniosoftware/invenio-pidstore/pull/31

Note "accounts" but "record".

Note "accounts" but "user".

Proposed new convention: `tablename = modulename_*` which would call for using `records` and `editor` prefixes for tables, to take two examples.  This would align table naming convention to our [our module naming convention](http://pythonhosted.org/invenio/architecture/module-anatomy-naming.html).

CC @jirikuncar @lnielsen @nharraud

We need the ability to destroy a valid session without the user making a request (this is useful to e.g. ban users immediately #44). Also, this can be used to allow users to see a list of their active sessions (e.g. from multiple devices, and logout a specific device). This is only really possible when sessions are stored server-side (i.e #51). We also need to associate a session (for a logged in user) with a user id (Flask-Session/KVSession use uuids as session ids).

- [ ] Model: user, session, timestamp (UTC), country, browser, os (deduced from ip and user agent)
- [ ] Connect to Flask-Login's ``user_logged_in`` signal to store the session activity when a user logins.
- [ ] Admin interface (needs very restrictive permissions). Browse, remove, filter by user. (related to #33)
- [ ] Celery cron task to clean up session activity table.
- [ ] Configuration: Allow 1) tracking only user/session/timestamp and not country, browser and os 2) completely switching off the session activity feature.
- [ ] Account settings item "Security", with a panel that list Sessions (see e.g. DropBox as an example). Allow a user to remove a session plus "Log me out of all devices". I would also include "Change password" on the same page.

issue_title: Library accepts some invalid tags as valid
issue_body:
An example, as shown in the changelog is 'en-mt-arab'.  This tag is invalid according to BCP 47 (it should rather be en-Arab-MT, if in canonical form).  The primary problem is that the grammar given in BCP 47 is not adhered to either in this library or in the javascript "ancestor" (at least as far as I can tell; I didn't look deeply at it because I'm interested in a Python implementation).  You may wish not to modify until or unless the upstream Javascript version is changed, but I think it is important to note that the library currently accepts invalid tags as valid.

A validator that correctly parses the above invalid example can be found at http://schneegans.de/lv/; it indicates that it is invalid, but a switch to en-arab-mt is correctly accepted.

If one could stream data between workflow stages, stages that now run in sequence could run in parallel.  User would designate some app inputs  and outputs as "streamable", meaning they can be a named pipe, and then you could run two stages in parallel on one worker instance, streaming data from one to the other.   (Or even on different instances, streaming through a socket.)  To work with the current API, you could take a workflow and automatically combine two or more stages into one, if the corresponding inputs and outputs are streamable.

issue_title: GS can't eat its own dog food.
issue_body:
**situation**
set up a plone 5.1a2 site.
create a snapshot in portal_setup.
change the day the week should start with in the control panel "Date and Time Settings".
create a snapshot in portal_setup again and make a diff between those two.

i then safed this changed settings to my registry.xml
```
  <record name="plone.first_weekday" interface="Products.CMFPlone.interfaces.controlpanel.IDateAndTimeSchema" field="first_weekday">
    <field type="plone.registry.field.Choice">
      <description xmlns:ns0="http://xml.zope.org/namespaces/i18n" ns0:domain="plone" ns0:translate="help_first_weekday">First day in the week.</description>
      <title xmlns:ns0="http://xml.zope.org/namespaces/i18n" ns0:domain="plone" ns0:translate="label_first_weekday">First weekday</title>
      <vocabulary>plone.app.vocabularies.Weekdays</vocabulary>
    </field>
    <value>3</value>
  </record>
```

now i set up another plone site and also install my package with this settings stored inside and get an error:
```
Traceback (innermost last):
  Module ZPublisher.Publish, line 138, in publish
  Module ZPublisher.mapply, line 77, in mapply
  Module ZPublisher.Publish, line 48, in call_object
  Module Products.CMFPlone.browser.admin, line 272, in __call__
  Module Products.CMFPlone.factory, line 168, in addPloneSite
  Module Products.GenericSetup.tool, line 379, in runAllImportStepsFromProfile
   - __traceback_info__: profile-akbild.site:default
  Module Products.GenericSetup.tool, line 1414, in _runImportStepsFromContext
  Module Products.GenericSetup.tool, line 1226, in _doRunImportStep
   - __traceback_info__: plone.app.registry
  Module plone.app.registry.exportimport.handler, line 47, in importRegistry
  Module plone.app.registry.exportimport.handler, line 90, in importDocument
  Module plone.app.registry.exportimport.handler, line 246, in importRecord
   - __traceback_info__: record name: plone.first_weekday
  Module plone.supermodel.utils, line 144, in elementToValue
  Module zope.schema._field, line 322, in fromUnicode
  Module zope.schema._bootstrapfields, line 183, in validate
  Module zope.schema._field, line 338, in _validate
ConstraintNotSatisfied: (u'0', '')
```
0 -> Monday (Europe), but every other number from 0 (monday) to 6 (sunday) generates the same error.

**why does that happen?**
the choice field (zope.schema) validates a given value (the one from the xml file -> u'0') against its vocabulary, using by_value, which is - in case of plone.app.vocabularies.datetimerelated.WeekdaysFactory - a dict of number (keys) and simpleterm (value) - pairs. but the value from the xml is a unicode string, not an int. -> so its not a key in the vocabulary.  see zope.schema._field.Choice._validate() for that.
```python
u'0' in {0:term_a, 1:term_b} == False
```

**further down the rabbit hole...**
most SimpleTerms in plone.app.vocabularies are instantiated by a value (string) and a title (term); only the Month and Weekday Vocabularies are build different. the come with a value, token and title. value is an index, token a string representation of that index and the title (term). this is, at least, inconsistent. 

**solutions**
1. rewrite the vocabularies to have better key/value consistency and fix cmfplone Products.CMFPlone.setuphandlers.first_weekday_setup to use strings instead of integers.
2. extend the choice field to also handle value_type; like IDict and ICollection does.
3. make all GS setup files JSON instead of XML (no... just kidding... but would be awesome)

i will implement the first solution, as i am not able (too heavy) to extend the Choice field or reimplement the whole GS-setup infrastructure.

take a look at:
https://github.com/plone/Products.CMFPlone/issues/1794
and
https://github.com/plone/plone.app.vocabularies/issues/41

sadly they are cross depending ...

This is because RootView doesn't have `permission_classes` declared, and `rest_framework.permissions.DjangoModelPermissionsOrAnonReadOnly` is my default permissions class in `settings.REST_FRAMEWORK['DEFAULT_PERMISSION_CLASSES']`.

Adding the following to RootView solves it:
```
    permission_classes = (
        permissions.AllowAny,
    )
```

issue_title: Read & write to entire SD card is an unreasonable default permission for most games and basic UI applications
issue_body:
Read & write to entire SD card is an unreasonable default permission for most games and basic UI applications. A game can easily just store savegames wherever it is supposed to store its own specific settings (other games do without sd card access just fine and offer savegames, so I assume this works) and a UI app that does whatever, maybe just provide a remote control UI for something, often will not need SD card access either.

Even for apps that have a good reason to write/read from anywhere, it makes a much more trustworthy impression to ask for this when an actual import/load/... button is pressed (where a user could even possibly decline), instead of just requiring this permission right from the start - which I assume is possible to do with `pyjnius`, at least in theory.

Therefore, I think `p4a` should be changed to package apps per default without this permissions. Also, until that happens, is there a way I can remove this permission manually?

issue_title: Behaviour change of numexpr; NaN instead of 0
issue_body:
Hi,

I am not sure whether this is a `numexpr` issue, or rather a `mkl` or even a `numpy` issue. However, I think the most likely case is `numexpr`.

My pytest CI suite threw a lot of errors in my last run. Digging into it I could boil it down to this minimal example:

    import numpy as np
    from numexpr import evaluate
    test = np.array([-1-1j])*np.infty
    out1 = np.exp(test)
    out2 = evaluate("exp(test)")
    print(' PRINT OUT RESULT ::', out1, out2)

Doing this evaluation with `numpy`, hence `np.exp(test)`, results in `[ 0.-0.j]`. This result was also returned by `numexpr`, until recently. Now, `numexpr` returns `[nan+nanj]`.

I attach below the result for the above minimal example  (saved as `test_numexpr.py`). I run the test first with the up-to-date conda, where `numpy` and `numexpr` yield different results. Then I downgrade `mkl` from 2018 to 2017, which also slightly downgrades `numexpr`. With the older versions `numexpr` and `numpy` yield the same result:

    test/> python test_numexpr.py
    PRINT OUT RESULT :: [ 0.-0.j] [ nan+nanj]

    test/> conda install mkl=2017

    Fetching package metadata ...............
    Solving package specifications: .

    Package plan for installation in environment ~/anaconda3/envs/test_36:

    The following packages will be DOWNGRADED:

        mkl:     2018.0.0-hb491cac_4   --> 2017.0.4-h4c4d0af_0
        numexpr: 2.6.2-py36hdd3393f_1  --> 2.6.2-np113py36_0  
        numpy:   1.13.3-py36ha12f23b_0 --> 1.13.1-py36_0      
        scipy:   1.0.0-py36hbf646e7_0  --> 0.19.1-np113py36_0 

    test/> python test_numexpr.py
    PRINT OUT RESULT :: [ 0.-0.j] [ 0.-0.j]

Any ideas on that?

Thanks,
Dieter

issue_body:
Hello! I'm a maintainer of [Trio](https://trio.readthedocs.io), an async library for Python. (Think of it as an alternative to asyncio.) We just had someone try to use it and raven together, and they ran into some issues: https://github.com/python-trio/trio/issues/469

I'm not sure what the best way to fix these is, and it might require changes on both sides, so I figured it was worth starting a conversation. There are a few different things.

# Raven crashes when trying to process Trio program stack frames

Trio, for extremely annoying reasons related to limitations in the Python interpreter and control-C handling, has to store some metadata inside magic invisible local variables that it injects into some user frames  [1]. In order to make sure that we don't collide with actual user local variables, we do this by storing an entry in the `frame.f_locals` dict with a unique sentinel object as its key, i.e., it's a local variable but its name is not a string. Yeah, Python allows this – who knew, right? Well... apparently raven didn't :-). It makes raven crash. On the one hand, trio's behavior here is The Right Thing, and raven might want to be robust against this in general, given that Python does allow it. OTOH, if raven is breaking then probably other things will also break, and maybe I should just hold my nose and name the variable `__trio_internal_value_please_ignore_this_and_dont_name_any_variables_like_this_awoiehiuawf` or whatever.

# Trio's extensions to Python's extension semantics cause problems with/for Raven

This is more interesting. Trio is a concurrency library, and one of the fun advantages of concurrent programs over sequential programs is that in a concurrency library, you can have multiple things crash at the same time. Most (all other?) concurrency libraries for Python handle this by throwing some exceptions on the floor and hoping for the best. (I guess you of all projects are aware of this!) Trio doesn't throw exceptions on the floor; instead it arranges its tasks in a tree, and if one task doesn't handle an exception it flows up into its parent task, etc., until it eventually hits the root of the whole program. But this means that you have to handle the case where two exceptions are propagating independently and bump into each other. Python... doesn't really have any way to handle this, but we refuse to throw exceptions on the floor, so we're stuck hacking around Python's limitations.

Basically the way we do this is with a [`MultiError` class](https://trio.readthedocs.io/en/latest/reference-core.html#working-with-multierrors), which is an `Exception` class that acts as a container for other exceptions. It's actually fairly sophisticated: you can have `MultiError`s nested inside each other, and the nested structure represents the nested structure of the task tree, we have mechanisms to catch "part of" a `MultiError` and trio knows how to fix up tracebacks afterwards, we can print tracebacks in a nice way where we first show the path that each exception took independently, then as they join up we switch to printing the common traceback together, etc.

There's a [simple example here to give you the idea](https://gist.github.com/njsmith/21691ce1b10c54f81aeaf87f43711959).

Of course, raven has no idea about any of this.

One issue this causes is that trio and raven both want to take over `sys.excepthook`. They're both written carefully, but since trio actually wants to replace the default handling of tracebacks, it can't do raven's trick of falling back on the regular hook; instead, if it detects that some other library has been missing with `sys.excepthook`, then it [prints a warning and skips installing its hook](https://github.com/python-trio/trio/blob/aee2676fd5096daf2849db9756242a30e0aa1f30/trio/_core/_multierror.py#L448-L457). End result: if you `import raven, trio`, then exception printing is broken. `import trio, raven` OTOH works fine. It'd be nice if we could coordinate to make this work both ways. Any ideas?

The other deeper issue is obviously raven has no idea about these special `MultiError` objects, so even after we get `excepthook` to run, it won't actually be able to report proper traceback information. Ideally raven should be peeking inside to gather up the full traceback tree, especially since the topmost `MultiError`'s traceback will usually be the boring stuff you already knew like "oh the error happened underneath `main` that's nice good to know", and it's the embedded exceptions that have the information on the frames where the errors actually occurred.

I'd love to hear any thoughts here.

-----

[1] If you really want to know the details, [there's an exhaustive/exhausting blog post here](https://vorpus.org/blog/control-c-handling-in-python-and-trio/#how-do-we-know-which-code-should-be-protected), and [bpo-12857](https://bugs.python.org/issue12857) is the upstream issue that I'm hoping we might be able to fix for 3.8. But in the mean time we're stuck with this.

issue_title: SPARQL queries being slow because of exhaustive search instead of Join/CTE
issue_body:
I'm new to rdflib and mapping of SPARQL-to-SQL. We're re-running the well-known Berlin benchmark (adapted version here [1]), starting with 27'886 Triples (scale1000.nt). Now looking at the log of the following simple Query 1:
```
  PREFIX bsbm: <http://www4.wiwiss.fu-berlin.de/bizer/bsbm/v01/vocabulary/>
  PREFIX rdf: <http://www.w3.org/1999/02/22-rdf-syntax-ns#>
  PREFIX rdfs: <http://www.w3.org/2000/01/rdf-schema#>
  SELECT DISTINCT ?product ?label
    WHERE {
      ?product rdfs:label ?label .
      ?product rdf:type <http://www4.wiwiss.fu-berlin.de/bizer/bsbm/v01/instances/ProductType2> .
      ?product bsbm:productFeature <http://www4.wiwiss.fu-berlin.de/bizer/bsbm/v01/instances/ProductFeature12> .
      ?product bsbm:productFeature <http://www4.wiwiss.fu-berlin.de/bizer/bsbm/v01/instances/ProductFeature20> .
      ?product bsbm:productPropertyNumeric1 ?value1 .
      FILTER (?value1 > 348)
      }
  ORDER BY ?label
  LIMIT 10
```
.. rdflib obviously generates subsequent queries of about 15'000(!) times in case of scale1000.nt.
This obviously becomes inacceptable slow, compared to native triple stores and Graph databases.

AFAIK it's easy to write a proper SQL query using join, union and ev. recursive CTE which would be much more performant and scalable.

Can someone explain if current behaviour of rdflib is caused by implementation issues (SPARQL-to-SQL-Mapper, OR-Mapper/SQLAlchemy), storage and/or architectural issues - or is this a fundamental issue of mapping SPARQL-to-SQL?

[1] Berlin Benchmark: https://github.com/geometalab/MSE-Database-Seminar-Triple-Stores/
[2] Queries: https://github.com/geometalab/MSE-Database-Seminar-Triple-Stores/blob/master/rdflib/queries.py

The following command fails in a way which is obviously in error. Additionally, it makes a lot of unexpected requests related to the inventory source that should not happen.

> tower-cli group modify --name=EC2 --description "has been modified"  -v

Another issue is that the following command does not work as it should.

> tower-cli job_template modify --name="Apache" --description "has been modified" -v

It sends `{'description': u'has been modified', 'job_type': u'run', 'name': u'Apache'}` in the PATCH request. The `job_type` field is an artifact of the way that tower-cli processes default values. In this case, it would override any other selection for the field in the process of modifying something unrelated. This is a silent bug that could disrupt other processes that the command is used in.

When using oauth this python package uses the pycrypto library. The problem is that pycrypto  stopped being developed four years ago and has not been touched since. This entails a number of issues, probably including security issues, but what got me started down this path was the dependency hell it landed me in. 

Since pycrypto is four years old it depends on an even older version of cryptography, and long story short this led me into pinning old versions of two other libraries than pycrypto that pycrypto depended on. 

But the story doesn't end there, which probably is a good thing considering for how long pycrypto has gone unmaintained. The software I'm building is kept in a docker container during development, a container running Alpine Linux. Alpine has switched from OpenSSL to LibreSSL and in the end it was impossible to both satisfy the old requirements of pycrypto and those of postgresql.

The solution to this issue is fortunately quite straight forward: Switching to a maintained fork named pycryptodome. 

In my limited testing this library works as a drop in replacement for pycrypto without any changes to the jira python library.

For further reference here is an issue against PyCrypto which has been linked by many others in the same situation as you: https://github.com/dlitz/pycrypto/issues/238

issue_title: Client library incorrectly returns error when FLAC is used with long-running-recognize
issue_body:
The client library incorrectly returns an error when anything other than LINEAR16 is used for a long-running recognize request:

[https://github.com/GoogleCloudPlatform/google-cloud-python/blob/master/speech/google/cloud/speech/sample.py#L173][1]

The v1 server support all encodings so this breaks functionality.

In general, the client library should almost never do it's own validation; it should always delegate to the server to be sure that that behavior and error messages are consistent across APIs.

[1]: https://github.com/GoogleCloudPlatform/google-cloud-python/blob/46e417e838001f4d1eb13f5e3fc2a54d390dd5db/speech/google/cloud/speech/sample.py#L173

issue_title: Pubsub message getting wrong attribute for publishTime
issue_body:
According the [REST docs](https://cloud.google.com/pubsub/docs/reference/rest/v1/PubsubMessage), a `PubsubMessage` has the field `publishTime`

In [message.py](https://github.com/GoogleCloudPlatform/google-cloud-python/blob/master/pubsub/google/cloud/pubsub/message.py), `from_api_repr` is getting the field `publishTimestamp` below:
```
instance._service_timestamp = api_repr.get('publishTimestamp')
```

The current tests are self-confirming of this issue as they simply set up the api_repr with `publishTimestamp`

A quick fix seems to adjust the following:
**message.py**
```python
    @classmethod
    def from_api_repr(cls, api_repr):
        """Factory:  construct message from API representation.

        :type api_repr: dict or None
        :param api_repr: The API representation of the message

        :rtype: :class:`Message`
        :returns: The message created from the response.
        """
        data = base64.b64decode(api_repr.get('data', b''))
        instance = cls(
            data=data, message_id=api_repr['messageId'],
            attributes=api_repr.get('attributes'))
        instance._service_timestamp = api_repr.get('publishTime')
        return instance
```

**test_message.py**
```python
    def test_from_api_repr_no_attributes(self):
        from base64 import b64encode as b64
        DATA = b'DEADBEEF'
        B64_DATA = b64(DATA)
        MESSAGE_ID = '12345'
        TIMESTAMP = '2016-03-18-19:38:22.001393427Z'
        api_repr = {
            'data': B64_DATA,
            'messageId': MESSAGE_ID,
            'publishTime': TIMESTAMP,
        }
        message = self._getTargetClass().from_api_repr(api_repr)
        self.assertEqual(message.data, DATA)
        self.assertEqual(message.message_id, MESSAGE_ID)
        self.assertEqual(message.attributes, {})
        self.assertEqual(message.service_timestamp, TIMESTAMP)

    def test_from_api_repr_w_attributes(self):
        from base64 import b64encode as b64
        DATA = b'DEADBEEF'
        B64_DATA = b64(DATA)
        MESSAGE_ID = '12345'
        ATTRS = {'a': 'b'}
        TIMESTAMP = '2016-03-18-19:38:22.001393427Z'
        api_repr = {
            'data': B64_DATA,
            'messageId': MESSAGE_ID,
            'publishTime': TIMESTAMP,
            'attributes': ATTRS,
        }
        message = self._getTargetClass().from_api_repr(api_repr)
        self.assertEqual(message.data, DATA)
        self.assertEqual(message.message_id, MESSAGE_ID)
        self.assertEqual(message.service_timestamp, TIMESTAMP)
        self.assertEqual(message.attributes, ATTRS)
```

issue_title: PubSub: Default thread-based subscriber Policy's on_response() implementation degenerates into series of synchronous calls because of debug statement
issue_body:
https://github.com/GoogleCloudPlatform/google-cloud-python/blob/4e17158c0f14e0e91cbeb74c3e8483c5a231e9ac/pubsub/google/cloud/pubsub_v1/subscriber/policy/thread.py#L147

While testing out the new PubSub API (v 0.28.3), I noticed that the default thread-based `Policy`, which uses a `ThreadPoolExecutor(max_workers=10)` seemed to be executing the subscriber callbacks sequentially, instead of asynchronously.  Upon introspection of the source, it looks like the above referenced code:
```
...
future = self._executor.submit(self._callback, message)
logger.debug('Result: %s' % future.result())
```
will block until `self._callback` completes, because of the call to `future.result()`.  Or am I losing my mind?  Thanks!

A `.pytest_cache` directory is created at the root of a repo using `1.4.0rc1` after python test failures. We should relocate this under `.pants.d`.

Running a pants command as `./pants -q <args...>` suppresses all output, but putting `-q` after one of the goals causes pants to display other workunit output except for that goal (e.g. `./pants run -q <args...>`). This could be useful, but it seems far more likely to be confusing that `-q` is accepted as an argument, but doesn't actually quiet all the output, and for the case of `./pants run -q <args...>`, the user almost definitely wants to have only the output of running the executable, e.g. for consumption in a script. It might clarify things a bit to deprecate the recursive behavior.

issue_title: Add a Task 'implementation version', to allow Task implementers to invalidate artifacts
issue_body:
The implementation version would be a (string) property of a Task that a Task implementer could use to invalidate all artifacts for the Task.

Additionally, in the case of `Task.incremental==True`, the implementation version should be used to skip incrementalism. The easiest way to do this would likely be to include the current implementation version in the `results_dir` path, and to _not_ persist it in the build invalidator.

issue_title: Allow pants to select a target by file(s)
issue_body:
There should be an option to accept literal files, and then select the targets that own those files. Similar to how the `--changed-parent` triggers a diff and the targets are selected based on the result.
The proposed syntax is something like:
``` shell
$ ./pants \
  --owner-of=this/is/a/file/name.java \    # flag triggers owner lookup
  --owner-of=this/is/a/file/name/too.py \  # flag triggers owner lookup
  compile                                  # goal
```
I'd be happy to start working on this right away 😄

Any comments, questions and tips on the implementation are welcome and appreciated.
👖 cc'ing @stuhood

issue_title: Add support for merging products produced by multiple promises
issue_body:
In order to support the example added by #2484, the engine will need support for merging products in some cases*.

Determining _which_ products are safe to merge is covered by #2525.

Currently, the native engine does not attempt to preserve Throw values that cross the boundary between python and rust. Part of the reason for this is that we (re)use a single buffer for the transfer of strings/utf8 data, and the completion of Runnables is batch-based.

To sidestep this issue and accomplish the separate goal of improved performance, we should have the rust code directly+concurrently invoke Runnables from `scheduler::Scheduler` via a new extern. This would replace the python `engine.Engine` class.

        except UnknownSystemEntity as err:

issue_title: New APIs to add/remove orgs and servers from sharing groups
issue_body: See: https://github.com/MISP/MISP/commit/9760f1144fcb13fb013124cef7e9424415b03966
`tasks.py` and `swift.py` call `os.chmod` and `sftp.chmod` with octal permission bits, but use the pre-Python 3 (also pre-Python 2.7.9) octal syntax (`0600` not `0o600`).

As hacked around in this patch:
https://git.openstack.org/cgit/openstack/cinder/commit/?id=3c3530db2445eca9be583715ff689bd746e6d387

Suds uses "/tmp/suds" to cache schemas -- in a multiuser environment this can lead to cache poisoning or simply being unable to write to that folder, and so the DFP API client doesn't work.

When you have a file containing readme.md (notice the case) it will not properly render the final README.md created during charm-compose.

Moved from https://github.com/juju-solutions/charms.reactive/issues/8

issue_title: Cannot use private registry with https url if get is restricted

This cause, that in the auth.py (expand_registry_url) will not recognise that the registry can speak https and therefore will not work with it.

The full trace is something similar to this:
I call the pull function of the Client class.
It will call the auth.resolve_repository_name(repository, insecure=insecure_registry) function.
This will call the expand_registry_url(parts[0], insecure), parts[1]

And in this function it will call the utils.ping('https://' + hostname + '/v1/_ping') function.
But this function knows nothing about the authentication. Therefore the server will return a 401 Authentication required. So this function will raise the following exception:
raise errors.DockerException(
            "HTTPS endpoint unresponsive and insecure mode isn't enabled."
        )

issue_title: Ignore "third state" of qubit in actual QPU samples
issue_body:
`dimod.response.infer_vartype` expects sample values to be binary `{0,1}` or `{-1,1}`, but the real QPU/SAPI response also includes the "third state" for missing qubits.

We can filter those out (in `dimod.Response.from_futures`) or support them (in `infer_vartype`).

It seems like `draw_chimera` requires numpy and matplotlib, but these dependencies are not listed in `requirements.txt`.  That said, both are fairly heavy dependencies for a simply graph layout.  It might make sense for this package to provide the `chimera_layout` function, which then can be combined with any viz tool (e.g. matplotlib) to provide the drawing dependencies.
The elimination_order module is a bit obscure, some module-level documentation would help direct users.

issue_title: is there any sample to code for dynamic bayesian network for 1-D data prediction ( voltage over the time)?
issue_body:
I have seen the kalman filter , I would like to develop the generic DBN to do the same stuff. but also work non-linear noise/error in data. here is my KF code.

import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

plt.rcParams['figure.figsize'] = (10, 8)

# intial parameters

n_iter = 500
sz = (n_iter,) # size of array

x = -0.37727 # true value (ref.)
z = np.random.normal(x,0.1,size=sz) # observations
Q = 1e-5 # process variance

# allocate space for arrays

xhat=np.zeros(sz)      # a posterior estimate of x
P=np.zeros(sz)         # a posterior error estimate
xhatminus=np.zeros(sz) # a priorior estimate of x
Pminus=np.zeros(sz)    # a priorior error estimate / noise
K=np.zeros(sz)         # gain or blending factor

R = 0.1**2 # estimate of measurement variance, change to see effect

# intial guesses
xhat[0] = 0.0
P[0] = 1.0


def kalman_filter():
	print "\n ------------- Kalman filtering process starts ------ \n"
	for k in range(1,n_iter):
	    # time update
	    xhatminus[k] = xhat[k-1]
	    Pminus[k] = P[k-1]+Q #  linear noise

	    # measurement update
	    K[k] = Pminus[k]/( Pminus[k]+R )
	    xhat[k] = xhatminus[k]+K[k]*(z[k]-xhatminus[k])
	    P[k] = (1-K[k])*Pminus[k]

	    print "x^-k:", xhatminus[k] , "\tP-k:", Pminus[k], "\tK[k]:", K[k], "\tx^[k]:", xhat[k], "\tP[k]:", P[k]

	print "\n ------------- Kalman filtering process ends ------ \n"

kalman_filter()

plt.figure()
plt.plot(z,'r-',label='noisy measurements')
plt.plot(xhat,'b-',label='a posterior estimate')
plt.axhline(x,color='g',label='truth value')
plt.legend()
plt.title('Estimate vs. iteration step', fontweight='bold')
plt.xlabel('Iteration')
plt.ylabel('Voltage')
plt.savefig('kalman.png', bbox_inches='tight')
plt.show()

issue_title: [Question] SimpleSeq2Seq Training Configuration related to OOM error
Add a check that all trainable parameters are actually updated
issue_body:
Idea from [this article](https://medium.com/@keeper6928/how-to-unit-test-machine-learning-code-57cf6fd81765).  This makes sure that you're model is hooked up correctly, basically.  The check belongs in [`ModelTestCase.ensure_model_can_train_save_and_load`](https://github.com/allenai/allennlp/blob/3c761ba9d857677efb60ae5c2dbbe321f0b3b702/allennlp/common/testing/model_test_case.py#L32), but it's a little tricky because currently that test has no access to the model _before_ training, only _after_ training, and this check requires a comparison of the two states.

Also, there may be some models in the future that want to disable this check, e.g., because they are doing multi-task learning and only some of the model is trained at any particular point.  So if/when we add this, it'd be nice if it were also easy to disable it for any particular model's test.

issue_title: Raise exception when using illegal LpVariable names
issue_body:
There are several LpVariable name characters which are silently converted to underscores:
`# illegalChars = "-+[] ->/"`

Creating two variables which contain these characters in the name can inadvertently become duplicate variables.  e.g. variables named "x+" and "x-" are both mapped to "x_".   Duplicate variable names are not handled well by all the solvers, and sometimes fail without any clear error reporting.

I suggest that illegal variable names throw an exception instead of converting to underscores.

The following code fails:
```
problem = LpProblem("fail", LpMinimize)
variableNames = ['x+','x-']
vars = [LpVariable(name) for name in variableNames]
problem += lpSum(vars)
problem += vars[0] >= 0
problem += vars[1] >= 0
problem.solve()
```

with the exception
```
/usr/local/lib/python3.4/site-packages/pulp/solvers.py in solve_CBC(self, lp, use_mps)
   1344                                     self.path)
   1345         if not os.path.exists(tmpSol):
-> 1346             raise PulpSolverError("Pulp: Error while executing "+self.path)
```

The same code succeeds as expected if
`variableNames = ['x+','y-']`

Setting the following flag improves the error message somewhat, although the cause is still unclear
`LpSolverDefault.msg = 1`

results in 
```
Coin0008I MODEL read with 4 errors
There were 4 errors on input
** Current model not valid
```
A workaround for the user is to escape the variable names before creating the LpVariables with something like:
```
def escape(s):
    # illegalChars = "-+[] ->/"
    s = s.replace("-","(hyphen)")
    s = s.replace("+","(plus)")
    s = s.replace("[","(leftBracket)")
    s = s.replace("]","(rightBracket)")
    s = s.replace(" ","(space)")
    s = s.replace(">","(greaterThan)")
    s = s.replace("/","(slash)")
    return s
```

issue_title: NB Power forecasts broken with the change back to standard time
issue_body:
Here I thought "AD" in the .csv data meant "Anno Domini" and the timestamp was overly-specific about being after the the year 0. Here's a snippet of the problem from http://tso.nbpower.com/reports%20%26%20assessments/load%20forecast/hourly/2017-11-05%2001.csv

```
20171105000000AD,1293,0
20171105010000AD,1266,0
20171105010000AS,1261,0
20171105030000AS,1262,0
```

Turns out "AD" indicates "Atlantic Daylight Time" and "AS" indicates "Atlantic Standard Time" but including the letter "T" was deemed unnecessary by NB Power.

Fix `NBPowerClient` and add new unit tests for:
* time range entirely in daylight saving time
* time range of transition into daylight saving time
* time range entirely in standard time
* time range of transition into standard time

issue_title: Proposal: Get rid of `go printState` in deisctl
issue_body:
The `deisctl` command uses channels to send error and output messages, when it should just use `io.Writer` objects. Channels block too much, are the cause of a few past bugs, and force us to write an unnecessary number of goroutines to handle this. As far as I can tell, we're not gaining anything from using channels instead of writers.

In this proposal, I suggest that we switch to `io.Writer` instances instead of channels. We would usually hand `os.Stderr` and `os.Stdout` to a caller (instead of `outchan` and `errchan`). For testing, we would also be able to pass in different writers. 

Instead of doing `outchan <- fmt.Sprintf(...)`, we'd do `fmt.Fwritef(out, ...)`.

In the event that the underlying writer is not suitably synchronous, we can easily wrap in a synchronized writer.

issue_title: docs: Add documentation for recovering Ceph from quorum failure
issue_body:
The resolution to #2914 was a manual recovery of the cluster because it had lost enough monitors due to the addition of new hosts and could no longer achieve quorum. We successfully recovered the cluster with just one host that was part of the original, healthy cluster.

The line of responsibility for this documentation is unclear, as the relevant documentation exists in several places within the Ceph documentation, but we have no expectation that Deis users are familiar with Ceph (and arguably shouldn't be), so I think it'd be helpful to provide some general steps and examples for cluster recovery.

ping @mgartner - he wanted to see this, since I was silently performing these tasks without reporting in for several nerve-wracking minutes ;)

The general steps are:

1. Trying `ceph -s` on all hosts and noticing that none ever connect to the cluster - instead, they show timeouts and/or monitor faults
2. In a `store-monitor` container, using the [admin socket](http://ceph.com/docs/master/rados/troubleshooting/troubleshooting-mon/#using-the-monitor-s-admin-socket) to query [mon_status](http://ceph.com/docs/master/rados/troubleshooting/troubleshooting-mon/#understanding-mon-status), identifying that there are enough stale entries to prevent Ceph from gaining quorum 
3. Stopping the platform with `deisctl stop platform` so components stop trying to write data to store (note that leaving the routers up will allow application containers to remain up, unaffected)
4. Cleaning up stale entries in `/deis/store/hosts` so that dead monitors are not written out to clients
5. Updating `/deis/store/monSetupLock` to point to the healthy monitor -- note that this isn't strictly necessary, as this value is only used if wiping clean and starting a fresh cluster from scratch with no data, but it's good cleanup
6. Start the healthy monitor and use the admin socket to get the current state of the cluster. 
  * Given the cluster state as the monitor sees it, use [monmaptool](http://ceph.com/docs/master/man/8/monmaptool/) to manually remove stale monitor entries from the monmap (i.e. `monmaptool --rm mon.<hostname> --clobber /etc/ceph/monmap`)
7. On the same host as the healthy monitor, get a shell in a store-monitor container without a monitor running (see #2925), and inject the prepared monmap into the monitor with `ceph-mon -i <hostname> --inject-monmap /etc/ceph/monmap`)
8. Start the monitor and ensure it achieves quorum by itself (use `ceph -s` and/or query mon_status on the admin socket)
9. Start the other monitors and ensure they connect
10. Start the OSDs with `deisctl start store-daemon`.
11. Observe the OSD map with `ceph osd dump`
  * For each OSD that is no longer with us, follow [Removing an OSD](http://docs.deis.io/en/latest/managing_deis/add_remove_host/#removing-an-osd) -- take care to ensure that the data is relocated before marking another OSD as `out`.
12. Once the OSD map reflects the now-healthy OSDs, start the remaining store services in order:
  * `deisctl start store-metadata`
  * `deisctl start store-gateway`
13. Confirm that the cluster is healthy with the metadata servers added, and then start `store-volume` with `deisctl start store-volume`.
14. Start the remaining services with `deisctl start platform`
