issue_title: To much queries issued in a lun/snap attachment
issue_body:
In OpenStack, clone a volume and inspect the volume logs.

Use following command ``grep "req-dc8c8765-6d52-4aee-9a51-1b158c3a2794" /opt/stack/logs/screen/screen-c-vol.log | grep "https://192.168.1.58:443/api/instances/hostInitiator/HostInitiator_" > too_much_query.log``

Around 50 queries issued in the logs, and more that **40** seconds used

[too_much_query.zip](https://github.com/emc-openstack/storops/files/860606/too_much_query.zip)

### What is wrong?

I ended up creating multiple fast-and-dirty attempts to get Trinity to respect it's `max_peers` limit.  This needs to be cleaned up.  We *should* be able to enforce this with code at only a small number of code locations.

1. refrain from `self.connect(...)` when full.
2. refuse incoming connections when full.

### How can it be fixed

Take a look at all of the places where we check `is_full` and condense it down to only the necessary ones.

issue_title: Why use two different build scripts: build.sh and bld.bat?
issue_body: I've been trying to port a few of my projects to be built and distributed with conda and binstar, and I am confused as to why [conda supports](http://conda.pydata.org/docs/build.html) two different build scripts: `build.sh` and `bld.bat`. I understand that `build.sh` is intended to be executed on posix systems and `bld.bat` on windows, but why not simply have a single python build script (e.g. `build.py`)? If conda is being  used to build a package, python is available. Additionally, a single `build.py` seems like it would simplify things for the package maintainer -- they would only have to maintain a single build script.

issue_title: error in load_database.py prevents creation of users in 3.5 (pip)
issue_body:
Hi,
just started trying to use Scout and ran into a small bump. 

My setup: Fresh Install of Ubuntu Xenial in a Singularity Container with a minimal system, Python2.7 and pip 9 and the current stable release of scout from pip. 

When trying to create a user, I got an error saying that the global variable institute_name was not defined; this seems to trace back to line 55 in load_database.py where the method accepts the institute ID from the command line, and then tries to re-assign institute_id=institute_name, which does not exist in this context. 
Changing this to institute_id=institute_id fixes the issue for me. 

Best,
Marc

Create a file when you add a case with parsable info about the run

- sample id map with internal and external ids
- enough information to recreate the entry in HK
When using standard lessc an output file argument can be given (```lessc asd.less asd.css```), I ask for support of this feature in this port.

On the webpage of [pysam](http://pysam.readthedocs.io/en/latest/usage.html#extending-pysam), importing pysam API in cython require this:

```
from pysam.calignmentfile cimport AlignmentFile, AlignedSegment
```

This works in pysam=0.9.0 but after I updated to 0.11.1, I have been getting an error: 

```error: 'AlignedSegment' is not a type identifier```

I have been able to work around with:

```
from pysam.libcalignmentfile cimport AlignmentFile, AlignedSegment
```

Is this the right way to do it in pysam > 0.9.0?

I'm just playing around with GHDL and vunit and I noticed some un-expected (to me at least) behavior.  When I run vunit with the -p3 option, the tests run significantly slower.  Is this normal?

issue_title: Suffix selection bug if value is not in single quotes
issue_body:
It seems that there is an index selection problem in curator 4. 

Suffixes that end in `0` don't match.      In the following example, `value: -2016.30` will not match, but  `value: -2016.33` will.    As a hack, you can match the index ending in a zero using a regex: `value: (.*-2016.3[0])`

**1) Put some indices in ES:**

```
PUT /test-2016.30
PUT /test-2016.33
```

**2) create a curator snapshot action** 

```
actions:
  1:
    action: snapshot
    description:  Testing Suffix Issue
    filters:
    - filtertype: pattern
      kind: suffix
      value: -2016.30
      exclude:
```

**3) Do it**

```
├ $ curator --config localhost-config.yml --dry-run snapshot-suffix0-problem.yml  | grep remains
< nothing found >
```

issue_title: Curator unable to delete hidden directories (prefix with dot in linux)
issue_body:
We've encountered an issue with Curator 3.4.0 where it won't fail, even though it's unable to delete (or seems to ignore) hidden directories in Linux when they start with a dot. Curator will clear-up the metadata, but not the data in the indices directory. This is hurting us on .marvel-* indices especially where the default is '.marvel-%Y.%m.%d'.

This is against a ES 1.7.2 cluster (I'm aware this is now EOL).

issue_title: Boolean environment variables are not coerced
issue_body: For variables that are boolean as environment variables get rendered as strings in yaml which fails the schema validation. I think the right answer is to coerce those variables into booleans like we do with integers.

HowTo: Fixing "Cannot process argument transformation on parameter 'InstalledModuleInfo'"
issue_body:
When you get the following error: 
```powershell
C:\Program Files\WindowsPowerShell\Modules> install-module azurerm -force -allowclobber

C:\Program Files\WindowsPowerShell\Modules> install-module azure -force -allowclobber
# PackageManagement\Install-Package : Cannot process argument transformation on parameter 'InstalledModuleInfo'. Cannot convert the "System.Object[]" value of type "System.Object[]" to type "System.Management.Automation.PSModuleInfo".
# At C:\Program Files\WindowsPowerShell\Modules\PowerShellGet\1.0.0.1\PSModule.psm1:1772 char:21
# + ...          $null = PackageManagement\Install-Package @PSBoundParameters
# +                      ~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~
#     + CategoryInfo          : InvalidData: (Microsoft.Power....InstallPackage:InstallPackage) [Install-Package], # Exception
#     + FullyQualifiedErrorId : ParameterArgumentTransformationError,Validate-ModuleAuthenticodeSignature,Microsoft.PowerShell.PackageManagement.Cmdlets.InstallPackage
```

Do the following: 
* close the PSSession and open a new one with admin rights
* update PowerShellGet: 
```powershell
Install-Module PowerShellGet # update
```
* try again 
```powershell
install-module azure -force -allowclobber
```

issue_title: Allow solution+grade cells for free response coding
issue_body:
I often find myself wanting students to freely code and write their own tests in a cell. In the current version I have to create two cells:

1. A solution cell that has my version of the code.
2. A grade cell with `assert True` as a placeholder to grade the students work.

The problem is that I have to create two cells *and* the comments in the formgrader go with the placeholder cell, not the student's code.

If we allow a code cell (like a markdown cell) to be solution+grade, it would be become much nicer to do this type of thing. Also, our current UI allows users to have a solution+grade code cell, so the fact that is doesn't work is some manner of bug.

issue_title: Typos allowed in command line flags
issue_body:
I ran into a case where I accidentally typed the `--remove` flag incorrectly, and yet nbgrader still did the right thing:

```
jhamrick@db981d4ff1f2:~/class_files$ nbgrader list --remov                                                                         
[ListApp | INFO] Removing released assignments:                                                                                    
[ListApp | INFO] cogsci131 Problem Set 0
```

I'm not in principle opposed to it doing that, but it should at least give a warning saying what it's interpreting it as.
There's some race condition where they keep hanging on the JS tests and then timing out.

As decided in the dev meeting, there should be a strict pipeline between the various grading steps. Each app will assume there are input files that fit the path "<?name>/<?student>/<?problem_set>/<?notebook>", where "name" is a name corresponding to one of the workflow steps (e.g. "submitted", "autograded", "feedback") and that the output files will follow the same format. This path will be configurable (as well as the names of the workflow steps), but will be the same across all the subapps. Additionally, each of the variables that match the different parts of the path can be filtered by, which allows filtering by student, problem set, and/or notebook, as the instructor desires.

This will require making some changes to nbconvert (breaking apart the main convert_notebooks function to be more easy to override).

issue_title: OverflowError when trying to call match on big data
issue_body:
When I try to call the `match` method on large data, an `OverflowException` occurs. Here is a simple example : 

```python
import yara

# the capture size is 2.3G
with open('../captures/bg1.pcap', 'rb') as f:
    data = f.read()

rule = yara.compile(source='rule foo: bar {strings: $a = "lmn" condition: $a}')
matchs = rule.match(data=data)
```

Here is the result :

```bash
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "test.py", line 10, in <module>
    matchs = rule.match(data=data)
OverflowError: size does not fit in an int
```

After reading the file `yara-python.c`, I saw that the problem comes from the call to the function `PyArg_ParseTupleAndKeywords` (line 1372) and more specifically from the format chunk `s#`. Indeed, as specified in the Python documentation (https://docs.python.org/3/c-api/arg.html) :

>  For all # variants of formats (s#, y#, etc.), the type of the length argument (int or Py_ssize_t) is controlled by defining the macro PY_SSIZE_T_CLEAN before including Python.h. If the macro was defined, length is a Py_ssize_t rather than an int. This behavior will change in a future Python version to only support Py_ssize_t and drop int support. It is best to always define PY_SSIZE_T_CLEAN.

In order to fix this problem, I'll propose a simple pull request (just define the macro).

issue_title: Can't run the unit tests without an AWS account

Something went wrong getting my new AWS user set up, so currently I don't have a valid AWS ACCESS_KEY and SECRET_KEY.

This means I can't run the unit tests for this repository, since they all start by downloading stuff from S3 which, even though it's public (anyone on Amazon can access it), requires a valid AWS ACCESS_KEY and SECRET_KEY in order to get at it.

We should pull the test data out of S3 using HTTP instead of "s3://" URLs, or we should just add it to this repository.

issue_title: Debian: Make live directory readable by ssl-cert group
issue_body:
On Debian, programs that run as non-root have the group `ssl-cert`, for example `ejabberd`.

The `/etc/letsencrypt/live` is only readable by `root` and nobody else. This directory should be readable and executable by the `ssl-cert` group.

issue_title: Warn when multiple IP addresses for a requested name
issue_body: Right now, Boulder will attempt validation of just one of the IP addresses in DNS. It would be helpful if the client did a lookup and warned the user that it can't guarantee success if there are multiple IP addresses for a given name.

issue_title: Installer should only select domains covered by the cert
issue_body:
After getting my first cert I used the installer to install the cert into my Apache configuration. When requesting the certificate I only requests a cert for www.MYDOMAIN.com.

When I ran this command:

`./letsencrypt-auto install --cert-path /etc/letsencrypt/live/www.MYDOMAIN.com/fullchain.pem --key-path /etc/letsencrypt/live/www.MYDOMAIN.com/privkey.pem`

the installer offered me 5 hosts to install the cert for, including just the IP address and MYDOMAIN.com and all of them were selected.
I think it's great that the installer figured out all the host names of my server. But offering host names which are not covered by the cert is actually asking for trouble. I think the installer should look into the cert and select only the host names which are included in the cert.

issue_title: Simple function is not flagged as containing an int overflow
issue_body:
I'm trying to understand why the following contract isn't flagged as containing a potential integer overflow:
```
contract Test {
    function test(uint256 input1, uint256 input2) returns(uint256) {
        uint256 res = input1 * input2;
        return res;
    }
}
```
I used `--solc-args=--optimize` to get simpler EVM instructions.
It looks like Z3 is trying to solve the following constraints and returns `unsat` for the relevant `MUL` operation in the `integer` module `_check_integer_overflow` function:
```
[Not(ULT(calldatasize_Test, 4)), UDiv(calldata_Test_0,
      26959946667150639794667015087019630673637144422540572481103610249216) &
 4294967295 ==
 3951741217, callvalue == 0, Or(And(ULT(calldata_Test_4*calldata_Test_36,
            calldata_Test_36),
        calldata_Test_4 != 0),
    And(ULT(calldata_Test_4*calldata_Test_36,
            calldata_Test_4),
        calldata_Test_36 != 0))]
```
However if I try to initialize these constraints myself by setting all the calldata/value vars to `BitVec(256)` the solver *does* find a solution (as it should).
What am I missing?

issue_title: angr looks for Self modifying code even if not asked to.
issue_body:

I think the intention was that if asked to consider self modifying code, there were additional sanity checks to be made before really looking for (are memory permissions ok?, ...).

But in the current implementation, the code ignores the value of `self._support_selfmodifying_code` and considers self modifying code whenever possible

```python
        smc = self._support_selfmodifying_code
        if state:
            try:
                p = state.memory.permissions(addr)
                if p.symbolic:

                else:

            except: # pylint: disable=bare-except

```

issue_title: DiracFiles in inputfiles should not be labelled as inputdata in Dirac
issue_body:
When an LFN is listed in the inputsandbox in the Dirac API, the file pointed to is copied to the PWD on the worker node when the job starts. This is waht happens if a `DiracFile` is used a an inputfile for the `Dirac` backend. However, in the line 

https://github.com/ganga-devs/ganga/blob/8ca6ee73653e32b272d4d5055187d1892f5558e3/ganga/GangaDirac/Lib/RTHandlers/DiracRTHUtils.py#L148

we set it as `inputdata` in the Dirac API as well. This seems wrong. It will cause the job just to be matched to sites where the LFN is present, but that seems wrong in this case (it is typically not a datafile). I suggest to delete the above line.
Some plugins, like kinto-attachment, would require to some cleaning where the server is flushed. Currently there is no way for the plugin to be notified.

`{"info": {"argument": "url", "type": "argument_required", "description": "Required argument is missing: url"}, "type": "BadOption", "error": 400, "description": "Incorrect HTTP API arguments"}`

getting this error with this splash request like this:

```
start_urls = ["http://www.gittigidiyor.com/"]

    def start_requests(self):
        for url in self.start_urls:
            yield SplashRequest(url, self.parse, args={'wait': 1.5, 'http_method': 'POST'}, endpoint='render.json')
```

It seems that majority of discovery handlers are run in blocking mode ("foreground" words in https://github.com/home-assistant/netdisco/blob/master/netdisco/discovery.py#L20), sequentially. And now the current duration for such blocking scan in various services seems to be 5s, and with 5 such services already, the single iteration takes 25s. I'd say that's too much, and may hurt user experience, if used in e.g. HomeAssistant. Discovery in HA is itself not much useful (https://github.com/home-assistant/home-assistant/issues/9135), but can at least serve as a "display hack" of "Whoa, HA automatically found my devices [now I need to go and configure them manually]". But with the delays like above, it can turn into "It takes half and ~~hour~~ minute for HA to recognize my devices after start, HA is slow, slow, slow!!!11".

So, as a first step to remedy problem, https://github.com/home-assistant/netdisco/pull/141 uses the duration of 3s. I'd say that actually too much for "local LAN" still, and I'd propose to set the default duration as 2s for all current services. Even better, set default as 2s, and let the caller specify max duration to be used for each individual service. Then, a small value (e.g. 0.5s) can be used on first call to quickly pick up majority of device after startup, and later, larger durations can be used for slowpoke devices.

ripal-pm

issue_title: Alpine Linux & Musl libc compatibility: _IOC_SIZEBITS undefined
issue_body:
I am experimenting with OpenThread in Docker using Alpine Linux, which uses musl libc. Compiling the unmodified sources resulted in an error about _IOC_SIZEBITS not being defined. Googling this error led me to the following page and prompted my solution included below: https://www.spinics.net/lists/linux-spi/msg08503.html

```
--- spi-hdlc-adapter.c    2018-05-06 16:36:19.000000000 +0200
+++ spi-hdlc-adapter.c  2018-05-15 19:14:09.000000000 +0200
@@ -50,6 +50,7 @@
 #include <sys/ioctl.h>
 #include <sys/file.h>

+#include <linux/ioctl.h>
 #include <linux/spi/spidev.h>

 #if HAVE_EXECINFO_H
```

I'm open to submitting this change as a PR, but maybe it's simpler for someone with pushing rights to just make this commit. Either way is fine, as long as this proposed change is the correct thing to do. ;-)

issue_title: Los cálculos de indicadores deberían ser a prueba de datasets con metadata errónea
issue_body: ```https://raw.githubusercontent.com/datosgobar/libreria-catalogos/master/energia/data.json``` En el data.json de Energía hay algún dataset que no tiene `accrualPeriodicity`. Este es un dataset de "metadata errónea" que debería figurar en el conteo del indicador de cantidad de datasets con metadata errónea. El cálculo de los indicadores que dependen de metadata de datasets, no debería romperse por errores en la metadata de un dataset. Este error debería atraparse con un `try: except:` y seguir calculando el indicador sin contar ese dataset. En el caso actual, con que haya un sólo dataset de metadata errónea se anula todo el cálculo de indicadores del catálogo.

issue_title: using tflearn for large dataset pixelwise training
issue_body:
I'd like to do pixelwise training in tflearn, but I'm having trouble understanding how to handle the labels.  Specifically, I want to train up an FCN on the spacenet dataset, so 7000+ 1024x1024x8 images.  Each input image will have a corresponding label that is 1024x1024x1, with the channel range being an integer, not binary.  I will start off with L2 loss, so I won't do any other image or label manipulation.

This data set is too big to put all in memory- my understanding is that Image PreLoader can handle piping in input, but it only specifies class_id for label, not a path to an image label.  I know I can flatten the image label into a vector, but it won't be one-hot.  I've gone through the TFLearn docs and several example programs, but everything I've found assumes one-hot labels.

issue_title: Make `-v2` less verbose or `-v1` more verbose for tasks
issue_body:
For external tasks, what bothers me is when I run (in simulation workflows) there is a huge number of this type of line generated:

```
INFO: bd37bb9ac1c8cbc1f4e89212a2a0d50f queued
```

which, unlike `start`/`completed` prompts that only show up as the workflow progresses. I tried to use `-v1` but that silences everything (no progress bar). These `queued` lines dont look very useful. I'm wondering if we can replace those `queued` lines with a line of summary showing the number of jobs queued, eg. "N jobs queued"?

Currently tasks are executed as isolated blocks of statements that do not return any value to the step. Suppose we want to perform some tasks sequentially and stop the step under certain conditions, e.g.

```
input: for_each='rep'
stop_if( sigma < 0.005)
task: concurrent = False
    simulation to calculate sigma
```
there should be someway for us to return variables to sos, something like
```
input: for_each='rep'
stop_if( sigma < 0.005)
task: concurrent = False, shared='sigma'
sh: 
    simulation to calculate sigma
sigma = float(open(result).read())
```

where SoS would get the value of variable `sigma` from the task namespace, and return to sos.

Another example would be running a large number of tasks and calculate statistics of some quantity, in which case tasks could be executed in parallel.

Of course the biggest issue is that there can be many instances of `task` so the results should be a list, or a dictionary with `_index` as keys. Then, if we are to expose the result to the entire workflow, another `shared` option would be needed at the step level.

issue_body:
Currently our `%preview` magic can preview `.dot` files containing one figure ([source code](https://github.com/vatlab/SoS/blob/master/src/sos/preview.py#L232), [example](https://vatlab.github.io/sos-docs/doc/documentation/SoS_Syntax.html#Mixed-style-workflows--175)).

With the implementation of #888, `.dot` file generated by sos would contain multiple graphs showing the evolution of DAG during the execution of workflows. It would be great if we could preview them as animations (`.gif`), so I believe that we can generate multiple `.png` files using the existing code, and generate `.gif` from these png files.

I look forward to demonstrating SoS using

```
%preview dag.dot
%run -d dag.dot
WORKFLOW
```

Note that displaying gif in Jupyter is possible, but might need some tricks. See Calysto/octave_kernel#105 and ipython/ipython#10045.

@HenryLeongStat , let me know if you are interested in this ticket.

issue_title: torchvision.transforms.Scale should be renamed to torchvision.transforms.Resize
issue_body:
Scale/Rescale implies a change by a factor X

Scale(X) usually means that you change the intensity of the image from I to I*X or size of the image from S to S*X

However in pytorchvision, Scale, is defined as:
Desired output size. If size is a sequence like (h, w), output size will be matched to this. If size is an int, smaller edge of the image will be matched to this number. i.e, if height > width, then image will be rescaled to (size * height / width, size)

The above definition matches better the concept of resize in image processing instead of rescale. For more information please see the following link.

http://scikit-image.org/docs/dev/auto_examples/transform/plot_rescale.html

I have installed PyTorch 0.3.1, torchvision 0.2.0, torchnet 0.0.1, visdom 0.1.7, CUDA9.0, CUDNN 7.0. I wrote some code by using torchnet to train my model, but I found the `FashionMNIST` val images are wrong, and the GPU memory leakage problem, I can't figure out why, I think it maybe a bug of `FashionMNIST` or `DataLoader`. 1. Run train from terminal, from this screenshot you could see the fashion mnist data set have been downloaded. 2. The used GPU memory at the first epoch, from this screenshot you could see the used GPU memory is **755MB**. 3. After one epoch, the used GPU memory increased, from this screenshot you could see the used GPU memory is **1913MB**! And I used visdom to see the val images, but you can found that the val images are MNIST not FashionMNIST! I have tested it on Ubuntu16.04 single GPU or Multi GPUs and macOS, only Ubuntu have this problem, the macOS haven't this problem. the reproductive code are here: [bugs.zip](https://github.com/pytorch/vision/files/1799976/bugs.zip)

[Love Streamlink? Please consider supporting our collective. Thanks!](https://opencollective.com/streamlink/donate)

I looked at `psparser.py` file and sow `bytesindex` function which purporse is to replace `byteobj[from:to]` and more precisely `byteobj[idx]` to return the same on Python 2 and 3.

From my understanding and experiments the only difference between Python 2 and 3 in regards to getting element or slice from byteobject is that in Python 3 when you get single element you receive integer instead of bytestrig. When you get slice is the same in both Python 2 and 3.

Now the implementation of `bytesindex` differ from how slices works if `to` is a negative value. In current implementation if `-1` (or any other negative value) is passed as `to` then it will be to the end of the bytestring instead to the end minus one byte (or the exact number of bytes). Because of that implementation detail all usages of `bytesindex` where all bytes to the end of the bytestring need to be get are misleading because it uses `-1` as argument.

The possible performance improvements can be because of reduced function calls if proper slice is used instead of the function. Also will be more obvious the the reader what exactly data are get from the bytestring.

issue_title: Interpretation of graph-valued and tensor-valued attributes underspecified
issue_body:
For graph-valued attributes:
1. Does the name of the graph have significance, and if so, how is it referenced?
1. Do the nodes in the graph valued attribute have access to the same Value namespace as the parent graph?
1. What scenarios are we trying to satisfy with graph-valued attributes? Are they meant to be a poor-man's function definition?  Are they meant to allow operators to work as scheme/lisp-style special forms?

For tensor-valued attributes:
1. Is the tensor value available as a Node/Graph input or output?
1. If so, is it referenced by TensorProto.name or AttributeProto.name?

issue_title: Can we have an option to export static graph?
issue_body:
When using pytorch 0.4, export resnet to onnx, the FC layer will be something like

 %169 : Float(1, 512, 1, 1) = onnx::AveragePool[kernel_shape=[7, 7], pads=[0, 0, 0, 0], strides=[1, 1]](%168), scope: ResNet/AvgPool2d[avgpool]
  %170 : Float(1, 512) = onnx::Flatten[axis=1](%169), scope: ResNet
  %171 : Float(1, 6) = onnx::Gemm[alpha=1, beta=1, broadcast=1, transB=1](%170, %101, %102), scope: ResNet/Linear[fc]
  return (%171);

Latest version of pytorch save it as 
  %190 : Float(1, 512, 1, 1) = onnx::AveragePool[kernel_shape=[7, 7], pads=[0, 0, 0, 0], strides=[1, 1]](%189), scope: ResNet/AvgPool2d[avgpool]
  %191 : Dynamic = onnx::Shape(%190), scope: ResNet
  %192 : Dynamic = onnx::Slice[axes=[0], ends=[1], starts=[0]](%191), scope: ResNet
  %193 : Long() = onnx::Squeeze[axes=[0]](%192), scope: ResNet
  %194 : Long() = onnx::Constant[value={-1}](), scope: ResNet
  %195 : Dynamic = onnx::Unsqueeze[axes=[0]](%193), scope: ResNet
  %196 : Dynamic = onnx::Unsqueeze[axes=[0]](%194), scope: ResNet
  %197 : Dynamic = onnx::Concat[axis=0](%195, %196), scope: ResNet
  %198 : Float(1, 512) = onnx::Reshape(%190, %197), scope: ResNet
  %199 : Float(1, 6) = onnx::Gemm[alpha=1, beta=1, broadcast=1, transB=1](%198, %121, %122), scope: ResNet/Linear[fc]
  return (%199);

The new one actually caused trouble for us. For embedded device, we want fixed size, and let TensorRT optimize the graph for deployment.  I don't want the "nice" dynamic feature in onnx.

It is possible to put an option to latest pytorch, so that we can choose to export a static and simpler graph?

issue_title: extent accept syntax to other fields.
issue_body:
Extend accept/reject logic to include matching with fields other than the file name.
This is a very common need, such as for report processing, which may involve interpretation of the 'from_cluster', and 'source' headers.

one possible syntax to accept messages with the 'from_cluster' header value of 'C':

match_header from_cluster,C

which does only direct matches.

a second possibility:

accept_header from_cluster,C

Where the argument after the comma is a regexp, and the accept clause is incorporated into the normal accept/reject chain of masks, merely applying to a different field.

An alternative syntax would involve no new directive, just an option on the existing accept/reject:

accept C HEADER=from_cluster

This would avoid the use of plugins for a wide swath of needs. Other suggested syntax welcome.

was: https://sourceforge.net/p/metpx/feature-requests/48/

issue_title: getEvents retrieves only up to 10 events
issue_body:
Thanks for writing this library. It's simple to use.

When calling calendar.getEvents(), the API only returns a max of ten results. The events aren't even the ones that are coming up next - they are scattered between ones today, ones in the future, and ones in the past.

It seems like filtering and/or paging needs to implemented to retrieve more. It's a bit difficult to tell, since it looks like this library is also using v1.0 of the API, which is deprecated, and will be disabled in Nov 2018. The Microsoft docs are all for v2.0 and different.

An icon for contacting via phone. Something like these:
https://www.google.com/search?q=call&hl=en&prmd=imvnsza&source=lnms&tbm=isch&ei=W8BXT77zJsiOiALKhPWFCw&sa=X&oi=mode_link&ct=mode&cd=2&ved=0CBUQ_AUoAQ&biw=1024&bih=1064#q=call&hl=en&tbm=isch&prmd=imvnsza&source=lnt&tbs=isz:i&sa=X&ei=XcBXT4LIOeSOiALJseTKCw&ved=0CA8QpwUoAw&bav=on.2,or.r_gc.r_pw.r_cp.r_qf.,cf.osb&fp=16abf2b338ddaa1c&biw=1024&bih=1064
